speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your life in Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow, I-- I-- I continued to do what I did at home. But also, I tried to be involved in business. And whatever my father did at home-- we had like representation from-- from-- from a, from a firm, from a manufacturer, and I went around to sell. And I did. I-- I did quite well. Started up, you know? As a matter of fact, that my, my brother followed me from Chrzanow. [LAUGHS] I made customers. We had a representation from, from, from a, a manufacturer, a big-- a good manufacturer. And we opened up in Cracow then a-- also a shop. And then I was also [SIGH] busy in the same-- the same way I did at home. I joined people there by learning. And that's how I spent the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you study in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radomsk shtibl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it located? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the main [NON-ENGLISH]. There were about 15. So it as, it was a few shtibls-- from Radomsk. You know, I'm talking about-- there were a lot of shtibls. But every-- there was a lot of Hasidic shtibls So mine was Radomsk. So I learned there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you lived? Where the address was, where you lived in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Cracow on the [NON-ENGLISH]. The number-- I don't remember the number. I went with-- I lived there with-- with a guy by a-- by a family named [NON-ENGLISH]. And they gave me space. I lived there with a friend, also, also from Chrzanow, which also departed to Cracow. He didn't want to go to the army. [NON-ENGLISH] was his name, from Chrzanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we lived together there. We rented a whatever-- a bed, a room, not apartment. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. What did I say was his name? [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], yeah. [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH]. And that's where I lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see your father? Did he come and visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My father came to Cracow, also, on business. My brother came, and I saw them, of course. You know, it-- I was in the correspondence, but I had to be away. And by the end of it-- shall I continue? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the end of it, [SIGHS] I was tired. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The reason I went to Cracow is because the way [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, to, to over, over, overgo the draft was to look for a doctor wherever, whatever, you know, they-- they-- they smeared him to get free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there till-- from, from '30-- '37, from '38, till the beginning of '39. And then I got tired. And I came back. I came back. And somehow we-- we contacted somebody who wanted to take money. But they-- they-- they-- they took me to the army. It didn't help. They paid. You know what I mean? I got-- I-- I couldn't stay long in Cracow. I-- I-- I-- I missed something at home. And they-- they-- drafted me. And I was in the army. From your hometown they drafted you? Or from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From my hometown. From my hometown, they drafted me. One was a physic, one was a doctor-- a military. And they gave me the qualification. They-- they drafted me. And they called me right away. The beginning of-- of-- of '39, when I came home, I stood to the-- to the-- to the draft. And a month later, they called me in. Usually, it takes-- takes a year, takes this year. But they called me right away. In March, I was already in the army. And-- and-- and-- and November was the war. In September was the war. So I was-- I was in active duty when the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you stationed in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a joke-- in Auschwitz. This was the [NON-ENGLISH], Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your position? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I-- I was in the army. And they called [NON-ENGLISH] which is heavy-- heavy equipped machine guns. In Polish, it's-- [NON-ENGLISH] is the word for heavy machine guns. And there-- there I was till-- till September.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to do with the machine guns? What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: How to use it. Well, it was-- it was-- it was distributed in in certain ways. One was carrying ammunition. One was carrying the gun. The gun was from two parts, one which-- which you put up. The other one was the real thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [SIGHS] sometimes, I was-- I-- I was given given the-- the-- the stand, which was the heaviest. [LAUGHS] That's what it was-- not always. How were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise, you have to carry two-- two boxes of-- of-- of ammunition to-- to-- to the-- to the position.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated as a Jew? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I had a good corporal. And sometimes I had a dog. Which means, in in in Jewish-- you know what I mean-- anti-Semite. And-- but overall, I held out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they treated you badly, what were the kinds of things that they did to the Jews in the army? An example.

speaker: interviewee
text: Called me names. You know, it was anti-Semitism, a lot of anti-Semitism. And-- but overall, I held up. I held up to that. But-- but-- but I didn't even-- I wasn't even long there. Because the war broke out in-- in September. I was from-- from March to September. We haven't finished yet. Because my father didn't go for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he had a doctor. He had a middleman which had connections to a doctor, a military doctor. And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] something-- they thought of something to pull me out. They gave me some-- some things to take. And-- and-- and the blood test should show that's something there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it didn't work. It didn't work. And-- and-- and then I-- in order, not only-- it helped me, but it-- it-- it made worse for me. Because I-- they-- they came-- they came under the idea that I-- I did something. And if it wouldn't be the war, I would have had to stand for-- for a trial at that time. But the war broke out. And everything was forgettin' -- forgotten. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you released from the army? Because of the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't released from the army. I was not released from the army. I was in the war. When the war broke out, I was in active duty. And I was marching. And I was marching with the army. But then was a battle, a big battle. And it was-- [SIGHS] well, they surrounded us. And I pulled out. And I hide near-- near a river. When they all passed by-- when they passed by, they occupied already, I changed the clothes. And I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where you were when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know where I was. I was-- from Chrzanow-- from-- from Cracow-- from Auschwitz, I came back to Chrzanow. Because Auschwitz and Chrzanow is-- is next to it, if you know you know about it. And from there, I couldn't stay there, because I was-- I was a deserter. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went. [SIGHS] And I-- I joined in Cracow. I joined a division. And we went to-- up to Tarnow. And I-- near Tarnow, there, I-- I-- I had-- a-- a-- a force, a fight was starting. And there it was a fight. A lot of-- a lot of people died there. But I-- somehow, I-- I-- I run out. And I hid. What was the group that you joined in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: The group?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- did it have a name? What was-- was it a resistance group?

speaker: interviewee
text: The army.

speaker: interviewer
text: The army again, you joined.

speaker: interviewee
text: The army-- in the army. I joined the army again. And I joined the army again. And I-- I-- they they send me to-- to-- to a-- to another division, whatever it was. And the-- the Tarnow-- and we marched till-- till-- on to Tarnow. And there, I-- I split.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I changed. I changed. And I came back home. I came back home to-- I came back home to-- to Chrzanow. My parents were already-- they-- they migrated from-- from Chrzanow to Cracow. We had a business in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, people migrated, because they ran from-- from-- from the Germans. Nobody knew that there is no use to-- to run. Because you run from-- from-- you run from one room to the other room. But that's the way it was. And plenty people got-- got-- didn't-- didn't-- didn't-- reach the-- their-- their destination. They were killed, or-- it was-- it was plenty to tell-- to tell about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my parents went-- moved to Cracow. And I came home-- and I came home from-- from when I [SIGHS] deserted there. When I when I came out, I came-- I went back to Chrzanow. And I went into my-- to our apartment. And I stood there. I stood there till I joined the parents back in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your sister and brothers also in Cracow now?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister was in Cracow. And the older sister was in Chrzanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was married, yeah. She had kids. Yeah. She was married. The younger sister, Regina, which I told you, she was in Cracow. And so I went to Chrzanow. And I went to our apartment. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then I came back to Cracow. At the time-- at that time, they-- they-- they confiscated everything in the home. Because whoever-- the first thing they they did is, whoever had business, they took all the business away. They-- they-- they forced me to open up shop. And they pulled out everything, pulled out all-- all the goods. Not only-- not only pulled out, but they gave me to carry it on trucks. Yeah, leather, balls of leather, 100-- 100 pound, or 100 kilo.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is still in your hometown, before you left for Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my hometown-- my hometown-- and the parents weren't there, even. So they emptied out the-- the-- the stock. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: They let you go, though? After you did that, they let you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: They let me go. They let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they do anything to the Jews in the town that remained? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, there was-- [SIGHS] next to Chrzanow, a name-- a place which called Trzebinia. Did you hear about this place? Five miles. Five kilometers, even-- less than five miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there, [SIGHS] when they arrived, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took-- what do you call it? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They took people. It's-- it's like-- like-- like whatever. They took whoever was on the way. They assembled them in a place. And [SIGHS] they killed them. As a matter of fact, if you look in the Chrzanow book, you will see a [NON-ENGLISH] with the names of-- of the people which they killed there. They just killed them. What-- what do you call it here, if you take one? It's a-- it's a-- Hostages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hostages. Took hostages. Hostages. They kept them there, and they killed them, put them off. That they now-- they killed them. And then in Chrzanow, they took also hostage. A friend of mine, which I went together to cheder, to school. And he was one of the most popular, because the father was the biggest [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what it means? Rich man. And they took him as a hostage, too. And they killed him, too. So I went back to Cracow. And I-- I lived with the parents.

words.text: I went together to cheder, to

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Cracow? Do you remember where?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] 15. It was mid-town, a good section. We had a business in Cracow. We had also a business in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't take away this business yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't. But-- but-- but we hid it. We hid-- we had to take out the merchandise and hide it. And then we left apartment. And it-- we-- we didn't have it. They-- they made judenrein in in Cracow. And they sent us to the ghetto. So they took it anyway. Whoever took it, took it. We didn't have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went from this apartment to the ghetto in Cracow, your whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] From there, there was a order that all the Jews had to-- had to leave Cracow and-- to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, we moved to Bobowa from Cracow. My brother was there at that time-- the younger brother-- the older brother-- one-- not the oldest. [POINTING] The oldest was-- was-- went to Lemberg, you know, at the other end of-- lives in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the same building there where we were was-- was-- was some people who-- who left Bobowa. So he says he going, too. So he was the one who was in charge. Because my father was already-- at that time, he was afraid to go out there. And he went there. And he rented an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bobowa was also a town, a small town. But the rabbi was there. Do you know the Bobowa rabbi? Did you hear about it? This was the town where-- where-- where we lived, we-- we moved. So we-- we-- we lived there. And from there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was '39. We lived there for a year. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the-- the ghetto came there-- not the ghetto. The-- the-- the Gestapo came there. And they lined us up. And they took us out from there. The young people took out-- all the young people assembled from Bobowa. And they sent us by train. So I was-- I was among them. Well, well, maybe-- When was this? Do you remember what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This what I try to remember. I missed something to tell you. I was with my brother in Bobowa, the one who rented apartment. And he used to go to Tarnow. Bobowa was not far from Tarnow. To Tarnow, and he had some way of-- of shuttling and making-- making-- we had to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: He brought from one place to the other, whatever. When he came to-- to-- so he went to Tarnow. And from Tarnow, he went to-- to another town. The name is [NON-ENGLISH] if you know it-- if you heard about it, no? [NON-ENGLISH], another bigger town. And he carried from one place to the other-- letters, money, whatever. You know what I mean? He made-- he made somehow-- and-- and he came back to the-- for the weekends. And he had-- he carried a white band, which means-- this white band gave him the permission to-- to go on-- on the railroad. Because he worked-- somehow, it was supposed to work for the government, for the Germans. So one day, somebody offered him there, in [NON ENGLISH], to go not to-- not to Tarnow, but in a town, Mielec-- Mielec what is the name, and bring something back. And he was not aware of anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he-- all of a sudden, he didn't come back for Shabbos. And it was very much disturbing. He didn't show up. We didn't hear from him. He didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, we heard, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with-- from-- from people who went with-- with him, that he went down the-- with the railroad in Mielec with two girls. And-- oh, at that time, it was already-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it? Blocked. Blocked with-- with-- with-- with-- with Ukrainian and with SS. And it was [NON-ENGLISH]. What do you call it? You know? So when they came down, they stopped him right away, and the girls, too. And according-- the girls came back to Tarnow. And my brother-in-law-- Regina, the sister, she-- she married to this guy, this Jacobovits. I told you, Jacobovits. He was in Tarnow. So he came back to my sister, to Bobowa, for Shabbos. So he [NON-ENGLISH] to the neighbors and didn't want to tell us this story, what happened He that he-- they gave-- they took him away. The girls, they send back. And the girls told the story that he says to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave him a shovel to dig. So he told them, I dig a grave. And this is what it was. So at that time, in Mielec. And he went-- he went to make a dollar, so he gave his life. At that time, was already at home. It was very much disturbing. And-- and my mother couldn't-- couldn't take it, my father. And I-- I couldn't-- couldn't go out no more. You know, I-- I-- I couldn't-- I couldn't face it, either. And this is how it was later. Things got tighter and tighter. They-- they always tighten the screws, more and more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you already wearing a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody wore a star. Everybody wore a star. But-- he wore-- he wore a star, but he wore a white band. But-- but this didn't help. When-- when they came to-- to-- to make judenrein, you know what I mean? In Mielec, this is what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this 1940, at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1942-- '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still in Bobowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940, we were still in Cracow. '41, we were still in Cracow. We-- we-- we-- we-- yeah. '41-- '42. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: They took all the young men away?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- in-- in March, '92-- '40-- '90-- '42. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: When they took you on the train, it was March of '42 as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: They assembled us. And they took all the young people on the train, on the train to Plaszow. We didn't know even where. But-- but I looked for a chance. And I-- and I jumped-- I jumped a rail. And I came to Bochnia. The town before Cracow was Bochnia-- Tarnow, Bochnia, Cracow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So I was-- I was blocked to go back. Because all the young people couldn't-- couldn't face no more my-- the-- the-- the town Bobowa. Because if they send us away, how-- what are you doing here? But then I heard in Bochnia that it's going-- something's going to come up in-- in Bobowa. So I-- I tried-- I got a a white band. And I took a chance, Thursday night. How did you get the white band? How did that happen that you got it?

speaker: interviewee
text: For money you got it. Yeah, you got-- you-- you could get it, you know what I mean? Was it kosher? Was it not kosher? You got it. That's all. You know, in normal times, they-- they-- they-- they-- they accepted it. But if you came a special [INAUDIBLE]. What's this? It was nothing. You're still a Jew. So where-- where was I?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're now trying-- you heard that something's going to happen in Bobowa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so I took a-- a white band. And I-- and I-- and I took a chance. And I went to-- to my-- to my home at night in order to get them out. So the mother says, Thursday-- where are you going now? Stay over Shabbos. I says, I can't stay over Shabbos. I want you to come out. Anyway, they stood. And I left. When I left, 5 o'clock in the morning, it was-- the rail was going 5 o'clock in the morning back to Bochnia from the way from Bobowa. It was-- it was-- it cacme from Jaslo, from other things. But the stop was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the railroad was already occupied with-- with-- with Ukraines, black. They stopped me. They took me to the station. And there were a lot of Jews there waiting already. They-- whoever in the morning, they assembled me, too. And-- and then, suddenly, I saw this commandant police, Polish, Laski. And he-- he was good. And he says to me, what are you doing here now? You're supposed to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I came home to take some things. Because I didn't have what do dress. And meanwhile, they take me here. So he put me into the train. He put me into the train. And I was the only Jew which came to-- to-- to-- to Bochnia. And the Bobowa rabbi's son was in Bochnia. And I was going in and out to there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I brought [INAUDIBLE], you know? I was the first-- first one. And this what happened. Then-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then I didn't have-- I-- I-- I-- I lived the life of a dog, because I didn't have a house where to go. And I didn't have the family. So I went back to Cracow. In Cracow, I had a sister. My sister, Regina, was in Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband was traveling, was leaving things in-- in-- in-- in Tarnow, come back. So I-- I looked for a place. I couldn't stay with her, because she had only one room with a baby, a small baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Months. And she-- she had her hands full.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I looked for a way-- where to stay. So my cousin, at that time, was-- was-- was a-- a-- what do you call it? [NON-ENGLISH] They-- they-- they-- they assembled the people to send them away, you know what I mean? Action.

speaker: interviewee
text: Action, yeah. Action. [NON-ENGLISH] in German. So they-- they-- they took his parents, my cousin's. So we-- a-- a few single-- single ones-- me and the cousin and another few boys-- they assigned us a room-- a room in an apartment. And this was a-- like three rooms, one after the other, going in from one room. And each one-- and we had the last room. It was five people, five boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the ghetto? Was-- there was no--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was still open, though? You could get in and out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where? In the ghetto? Couldn't get in and out. Yeah, you can't-- you find a way to come in. But-- but this was after the [NON-ENGLISH], after the-- after the action, there-- there was-- there was no Cracow. There was only a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, we stood there. We lived there for a short time. And the Gestapo came and looking around. They looked for-- for-- for-- what do you call it? Partisans, whatever-- illegals. And they knocked on the doors. And we put our lock from the outside. So it's means nobody's there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we came there. [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a-- a gestapo. He kicked the door. And it opened up. And we all spread in the room. One jumped the window. It was on the second floor. He broke a hand. They took us-- the rest of us, they took us and lined us up downstairs and took us to the Jewish police. The Jewish police, [NON-ENGLISH]. That's-- that's what they call it. And this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] September, October, '42. So they kept us there in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And a lot of things went-- happened there in this-- in this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of Jews collected there at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a full jail, all Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the area.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Jewish police, in the-- in the Jewish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a place inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the ghetto, there was a Jewish police. And on-- and they had-- they had like a barrack, which was a jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the next tape? OK.
